title: Lozenges and throat sprays
summary: Lozenges and sprays can provide short-term sore-throat relief but do not treat the underlying virus. Ingredient choice matters because anaesthetics, anti-inflammatories, antiseptics and zinc have different benefits and risks.
evidenceSnapshot: Local symptom relief is plausible and supported for some medicated products, but effects are temporary and formulation-specific.

interventions.name: Simple soothing lozenges
interventions.finding: Increase saliva and reduce dryness and irritation.
interventions.practical: Use as directed; sugar-free options reduce dental exposure.

interventions.name: Local anaesthetic lozenges/sprays
interventions.finding: Can numb pain for a limited period.
interventions.practical: Use label frequency and avoid eating immediately if swallowing feels numb.
interventions.cautions: Overuse can irritate tissue or impair safe swallowing.

interventions.name: Anti-inflammatory lozenges
interventions.finding: Some ingredients such as flurbiprofen can reduce throat pain.
interventions.practical: Ask a pharmacist if NSAIDs are unsafe for you.

interventions.name: Antiseptic products
interventions.finding: May reduce microbes in the mouth but have not been shown to cure a viral sore throat.
interventions.practical: Use for comfort, not as an antibiotic substitute.

interventions.name: Zinc lozenges
interventions.finding: May shorten a cold in some trials but causes taste disturbance and nausea; optimal formulation is uncertain.

practicalSteps: Match the product to the symptom: soothing, numbing or anti-inflammatory.
Check age restrictions and active ingredients.
Use pain relief and fluids as the foundation rather than stacking many throat products.
avoidOrCare: Do not give hard lozenges to children who cannot manage them safely.
Persistent severe throat pain despite numbing needs assessment.

myths.reality: The throat cannot be safely sterilised, and most sore throats are viral.

myths.claim: If numbness is stronger, the product is treating the cause.

redFlags: New swelling of lips/tongue or wheeze
Severe one-sided pain or neck swelling
unsettled: Head-to-head evidence between common Australian lozenge ingredients is limited.
